intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer visual calm and reduced brightness to feel less overwhelmed by recipe complexity
facade.label: Dark moody interface
facade.acceptImplies: Explore darker, muted palettes with softer contrast to reduce cognitive load during recipe browsing
facade.rejectImplies: Test brighter, more energetic interfaces that make cooking feel accessible and fun rather than heavy

evidence.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer visual calm and reduced brightness to feel less overwhelmed by recipe complexity
evidence.implication: Explore darker, muted palettes with softer contrast to reduce cognitive load during recipe browsing

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to irreverent, self-aware humor that validates their frustration rather than cheerful encouragement
facade.label: Sarcastic tone
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into witty, slightly cynical copy; poke fun at cooking complexity; use phrases like 'We won't judge' or 'Even you can do this'
facade.rejectImplies: Avoid sarcasm; use straightforward, encouraging, or neutral tone instead; keep personality warm but sincere

facade.hypothesis: Removing visual friction of ingredient lists creates a sense of effortless cooking, making the app feel like it's doing the work for you rather than asking you to manage steps
facade.axisTargeted: Transition behavior — does the interface reward action with immediate visual payoff or require the user to process multi-step information
facade.label: Ingredients vanish when added
facade.acceptImplies: Users want instant gratification and hidden complexity; the app should surprise and delight at each gesture, not make them track or verify inputs
facade.rejectImplies: Users want to see and confirm what they've added; the app should build confidence through visibility and deliberate, visible progression

facade.hypothesis: Warm, energetic mood might feel infantilizing or kitsch to people who hate cooking—they want efficiency, not joy-forcing. Testing if playful color/tone alienates or appeals.
facade.label: Cheerful yellow buttons
facade.acceptImplies: User wants approachable, positive energy despite frustration with cooking. Receptive to design that feels encouraging rather than clinical.
facade.rejectImplies: User wants neutral, serious, stripped-down interface. Playfulness reads as mocking their pain point. Prefer cool, minimal aesthetic.

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking want recipes presented like a deck—one big, touch-friendly card at a time, no scrolling through lists or navigating menus. Each card is self-contained: ingredient list, method, done.
facade.label: Giant recipe cards, swipe through
facade.acceptImplies: The app should prioritize a card-stack or carousel interface over tabs or sidebars. Gesture-based navigation (swipe) over click-heavy menus. One recipe per screen.
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer structured information architecture—tabs, sidebars, or list views where they can scan multiple recipes at once or drill down through categories before committing to one recipe.

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
Whether user needs heavy filtering (dietary, time, skill) upfront vs. minimal friction and discovery-based browsing
axes.poleA: Lots of filters or keep it simple

axes.label: Whether user wants instant gratification feedback (timers, notifications) vs. passive, low-pressure experience with no alerts
axes.poleA: Alerts and timers or silent mode

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user needs heavy filtering (dietary, time, skill) upfront vs. minimal friction and discovery-based browsing
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant gratification feedback (timers, notifications) vs. passive, low-pressure experience with no alerts
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking may prefer soft, approachable interface elements that feel less intimidating and technical than sharp, geometric controls
facade.label: Rounded pill buttons
facade.acceptImplies: Explore rounded, organic shapes throughout the interface to reduce friction and cognitive friction in recipe navigation
facade.rejectImplies: Sharp, minimal geometric forms (rectangles, squares) may feel more appropriate and less patronizing to this audience

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer a minimal, scannable ingredient checklist over narrative recipe prose
facade.axisTargeted: density vs narrative — how much supporting text vs pure data
facade.label: Just the ingredients list
facade.acceptImplies: Recipe app should lead with ingredient cards/list, minimize flavor copy and cooking philosophy
facade.rejectImplies: Recipe app needs engaging narrative, context, and storytelling to motivate users through each step

draft.title: QuickBite – Recipe App for People Who Hate Cooking
draft.summary: A recipe app that strips away complexity and delivers dead-simple meals in 15 minutes or less, with ingredient lists you can actually find and zero pretentious cooking jargon.
draft.html:
QuickBite

Meals so easy, you'll actually cook them

Filter by mood
All
15 min
No oven
One pan
Today's picks
Garlic butter pasta
12 min
4 ingredients
Sheet pan chicken & veg
20 min
6 ingredients
Egg fried rice
10 min
5 ingredients
